sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To create a service account or API (Application Programming Interface) keys for an organization, you
must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access to that organization.

To grant a service account access to a project, you must have
[Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access to the organization that owns
the project.

To grant API (Application Programming Interface) keys access to a project, you must have
[Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access for that project.

sections.heading: Optional: Require an IP Access List
sections.body:
When you create an organization using the Atlas UI, Atlas
automatically requires enables an IP access list for the
Atlas Administration API. This limits API (Application Programming Interface) requests to only those from
the location-based IP or CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) addresses that you specify in the IP
access list. If you make a request to the Atlas Administration API without
an IP access list entry, the server responds with a 403 status code.

If you disable this feature, you can make API (Application Programming Interface) requests from any
address on the internet as long as the IP access list is empty. Once
you add an IP access list entry, only requests originating from that
IP address can make requests.

> **Note:** [IP Access List](access-list) apply to *using* service account access tokens, not
> [cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token](generate-oauth2-token-atlas) or [revoking](revoke-oauth2-token-atlas) them.
> You can generate a token from any IP address, but you can only use it to call
> the API (Application Programming Interface) if your IP address is on the access list.

To set your organization to require IP access lists for every
Atlas Administration API request after organization creation, follow these
steps:

sections.heading: Grant Programmatic Access to an Organization
Create a Service Account for an Organization
sections.body:
Use the following procedure to grant programmatic access to an
organization by creating a service account and generating a new
client secret. To learn more about authentication methods for the
Atlas Administration API, see [Atlas Administration API Authentication Methods](api-authentication).

- Enter a Name.

- Enter a Description.

- Select a duration from the Client Secret Expiration menu.

- From the **Organization Permissions** menu, select the
  [organization role](organization-roles) for the
  service account.

  These roles determine the permissions of the access tokens
  generated by this service account. You use access tokens to
  authenticate to the Atlas Administration API when you [Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

  You can also assign project-level roles to the service account
  after you create it. To learn how, see [Grant Existing Service Account Access to a Project](invite-org-app-api-keys).

The client secret acts as the password when creating access tokens.

> **Warning:** *This is the only time you can view the full client secret.* Click
> Copy and save it to a secure location. Otherwise, you'll need to
> generate a new client secret.

- Click Add Access List Entry.

- Enter an IP (Internet Protocol) address or CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) block from which you want Atlas
  to accept API (Application Programming Interface) requests for this service account.

  You can also click Use Current IP Address if the host
  you are using to access Atlas will also make API (Application Programming Interface) requests
  using this service account.

- Click Save.

sections.heading: Generate an Access Token for your Service Account
sections.body:
After you create a service account, you can generate an access
token for it. You use access tokens to authenticate API (Application Programming Interface) requests
to the Atlas Administration API. For an example, see
[Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

To generate an access token for a service account:

Locate the client secret beginning with `mdb_sa_sk_` that you
saved immediately after [when created](see-org-api-keys), which was the only time you could view the
client secret. If you did not save the client secret, you must
generate a new client secret.

For example, run:

```sh
echo -n {clientId}:{clientSecret} | base64
```

Replace `{base64Auth}` in the following example with the output from the
preceding step, then run:

**Input:**

```sh
curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'
```

**Output:**

```sh
{"access_token":"{accessToken}","expires_in":3600,"token_type":"Bearer"}%
```

> **Important:** The access token is valid for 1 hour (3600 seconds). You can't refresh an access token.
> When this access token expires, repeat this step to generate a new one.

codeBlocks.content:
curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'

sections.heading: Create a Service Account for an Organization
sections.body:
You can use the Atlas Administration API to [create a service account for your organization](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-createserviceaccount).

After you've created the service account, copy and save the `{CLIENT-ID}` and
`{CLIENT-SECRET}` from the output, which should look similar to the following example.
*This is the only time you can view the full client secret.* You'll need this information
when you [Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

```sh
{
  "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
  "description" : "Service account for my organization.",
  "clientId" : "{CLIENT-ID}",
  "name" : "My Service Account",
  "roles" : [ "ORG_MEMBER" ],
  "secrets" : [ {
    "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
    "expiresAt" : "2024-12-01T00:00:00Z",
    "id" : "6627f7259d39d858378c9e30",
    "lastUsedAt" : null,
    "secret" : "{CLIENT-SECRET}"
   } ]
}%
```

codeBlocks.content:
{
  "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
  "description" : "Service account for my organization.",
  "clientId" : "{CLIENT-ID}",
  "name" : "My Service Account",
  "roles" : [ "ORG_MEMBER" ],
  "secrets" : [ {
    "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
    "expiresAt" : "2024-12-01T00:00:00Z",
    "id" : "6627f7259d39d858378c9e30",
    "lastUsedAt" : null,
    "secret" : "{CLIENT-SECRET}"
   } ]
}%

sections.heading: Generate an Access Token for your Service Account
sections.body:
After you create a service account, you can generate an access
token for it. You use access tokens to authenticate API (Application Programming Interface) requests
to the Atlas Administration API. For an example, see
[Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

To generate an access token for a service account:

Locate the client secret beginning with `mdb_sa_sk_` that you
saved immediately after [when created](see-org-api-keys), which was the only time you could view the
client secret. If you did not save the client secret, you must
generate a new client secret.

For example, run:

```sh
echo -n {clientId}:{clientSecret} | base64
```

Replace `{base64Auth}` in the following example with the output from the
preceding step, then run:

**Input:**

```sh
curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'
```

**Output:**

```sh
{"access_token":"{accessToken}","expires_in":3600,"token_type":"Bearer"}%
```

> **Important:** The access token is valid for 1 hour (3600 seconds). You can't refresh an access token.
> When this access token expires, repeat this step to generate a new one.

codeBlocks.content:
curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'

sections.heading: Create API (Application Programming Interface) Keys for an Organization
sections.body:
- Enter a Description.

- In the Organization Permissions menu, select the
  [organization role](organization-roles) for the
  API (Application Programming Interface) key.

The public key acts as the username when making API requests.

The private key acts as the password when making API requests.

> **Warning:** 
>
> Copy and save Public and Private Keys
>
> The Private Key is only shown once: *on this page*.
> Click the Copy button to add the Private Key to the
> clipboard. Save and secure both the Public and Private Keys.

- Click Add Access list Entry.

- Enter an IP (Internet Protocol) address or CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) block from which you want Atlas
  to accept API (Application Programming Interface) requests for this API (Application Programming Interface) Key.

  You can also click Use Current IP Address if the host
  you are using to access Atlas will also make API (Application Programming Interface) requests
  using this API (Application Programming Interface) Key.

- Click Save.

sections.heading: Create API (Application Programming Interface) Keys for an Organization
sections.body:
To create an API key in an organization using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas organizations apiKeys create [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas organizations apiKeys create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-organizations-apiKeys-create).

sections.heading: Add an API (Application Programming Interface) Access List Entry for the API (Application Programming Interface) Key
sections.body:
To create an IP access list entry for your API key using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas organizations apiKeys accessLists create [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas organizations apiKeys accessLists create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-organizations-apiKeys-accessLists-create).

sections.heading: Grant Programmatic Access to a Project
sections.body:
To learn more about these two authentication methods, see
[Atlas Administration API Authentication Methods](api-authentication).

To give API (Application Programming Interface) keys access to a project, you must have
[Project Access Manager](<Project Access Manager>) or [Project Owner](<Project Owner>)
access for that project.

sections.heading: Grant Existing Service Account Access to a Project
sections.body:
If you've already created service accounts or API (Application Programming Interface) keys for an organization,
you can assign them to a project to grant that project access to the
Atlas Administration API.

> **Warning:** If you assign an organization service account to a project, the
> [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) can manage the service account, including rotating
> secrets and updating the IP access list.

sections.heading: Step 1: Click .
Step 2: Locate your organization service account.
sections.body:
Start typing the client ID of your service account in the field,
then select your service account from the menu.

sections.heading: Step 3: Assign project roles to your service account.
sections.body:
In the menu that appears, select the [project role](project-roles)
for the service account.

sections.heading: Step 4: Click .
Grant Existing Service Account Access to a Project
sections.body:
You can use the Atlas Administration API to [grant an existing
service account access to a project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-addprojectserviceaccount).

sections.heading: Grant Existing API (Application Programming Interface) Keys Access to a Project
sections.body:
If you've already created service accounts or API (Application Programming Interface) keys for an organization,
you can assign them to a project to grant that project access to the
Atlas Administration API.

- Type the public key into the field.

- In the Project Permissions menu, select the
  [project role](project-roles) for the
  API (Application Programming Interface) key.

sections.heading: Grant Existing API (Application Programming Interface) Keys Access to a Project
sections.body:
If you've already created service accounts or API (Application Programming Interface) keys for an organization,
you can assign them to a project to grant that project access to the
Atlas Administration API.

To assign an API key to a project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas projects apiKeys assign <ID> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas projects apiKeys assign](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-projects-apiKeys-assign).

sections.heading: Add Project Access from a Project
sections.body:
If you haven't already created a service account or API (Application Programming Interface) keys for an
organization, you can create them for a project to grant that project
access to the Atlas Administration API. The service account or API (Application Programming Interface) keys
that you create for a project are automatically added to the parent
organization with the permission [Organization Member](<Organization Member>).

To create a service account for a project using the Atlas UI:

- Enter a Name.

- Enter a Description.

- Select a duration from the Client Secret Expiration menu.

- From the **Project Permissions** menu, select the
  [project role](project-roles) for the
  service account.

The client secret acts as the password when creating access tokens.

> **Warning:** *This is the only time you can view the full client secret.* Click
> Copy and save it to a secure location. Otherwise, you'll need to
> generate a new client secret.

- Click Add Access List Entry.

- Enter an IP (Internet Protocol) address or CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) block from which you want Atlas
  to accept API (Application Programming Interface) requests for this service account.

  You can also click Use Current IP Address if the host
  you are using to access Atlas will also make API (Application Programming Interface) requests
  using this service account.

- Click Save.

You can use the Atlas Administration API to [create a service
account for your project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-createprojectserviceaccount).

After you've created the service account, copy and save the `{CLIENT-ID}` and
`{CLIENT-SECRET}` from the output, which should look similar to the following example.
*This is the only time you can view the full client secret.* You'll need this information
when you [Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request](example-api-request).

```sh
{
  "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
  "description" : "Service account for my organization.",
  "clientId" : "{CLIENT-ID}",
  "name" : "My Service Account",
  "roles" : [ "ORG_MEMBER" ],
  "secrets" : [ {
    "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
    "expiresAt" : "2024-12-01T00:00:00Z",
    "id" : "6627f7259d39d858378c9e30",
    "lastUsedAt" : null,
    "secret" : "{CLIENT-SECRET}"
   } ]
}%
```

To create an API key for your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas projects apiKeys create [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas projects apiKeys create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-projects-apiKeys-create).

After you create the API (Application Programming Interface) key for your project, use the
Atlas UI to add an API (Application Programming Interface) access list entry. You can't use the
API (Application Programming Interface) key for the project until you set up the API (Application Programming Interface) access list.

> **Note:** 
>
> Atlas CLI Limitation
>
> You can't edit the API (Application Programming Interface) access list for a project API (Application Programming Interface) key
> using the Atlas CLI.

To add an API (Application Programming Interface) access list entry using the Atlas UI:

- Click Add Access List Entry.

- Enter an IP (Internet Protocol) address from which you want Atlas to
  accept API (Application Programming Interface) requests for this API (Application Programming Interface) Key.
  You can also click Use Current IP Address if the host
  you are using to access Atlas will also make API (Application Programming Interface) requests
  using this API (Application Programming Interface) Key.

- Click Save.

To create an API (Application Programming Interface) key for a project using the Atlas UI:

On the Create API Key page:

- Enter a Description.

- In the Project Permissions menu, select the
  [project role](project-roles) for the API (Application Programming Interface) key.

The public key acts as the username when making API requests.

The private key acts as the password when making API requests.

> **Warning:** 
>
> Save Private Key
>
> The Private Key is only shown once: *on this page*.
> Click the Copy button to add the Private Key to the
> clipboard. Save and secure both the Public and Private Keys.

- Click Add Access List Entry.

- Enter an IP (Internet Protocol) address from which you want Atlas to
  accept API (Application Programming Interface) requests for this API (Application Programming Interface) Key.

  You can also click Use Current IP Address if the host
  you are using to access Atlas will also make API (Application Programming Interface) requests
  using this API (Application Programming Interface) Key.

- Click Save.

codeBlocks.content:
{
  "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
  "description" : "Service account for my organization.",
  "clientId" : "{CLIENT-ID}",
  "name" : "My Service Account",
  "roles" : [ "ORG_MEMBER" ],
  "secrets" : [ {
    "createdAt" : "2024-04-23T17:47:17Z",
    "expiresAt" : "2024-12-01T00:00:00Z",
    "id" : "6627f7259d39d858378c9e30",
    "lastUsedAt" : null,
    "secret" : "{CLIENT-SECRET}"
   } ]
}%

sections.heading: Make an API (Application Programming Interface) Request
sections.body:
The Atlas Administration API uses one of two authentication methods to authenticate requests:
service account or API (Application Programming Interface) keys. You'll need the keys or secret that you saved when
[create a new one](create-org-api-key) to
complete the following procedures.

All Atlas Administration API endpoints have the following base URL:

`https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/<version>`

> **Important:** MongoDB uses HTTPS URLs to increase security. Using HTTP URLs will return a 301 status code.

To make an API (Application Programming Interface) request using a service account, use the service account to generate
an access token, then use the access token in your request:

Locate the client secret beginning with `mdb_sa_sk_` that you saved
immediately after [when created](see-org-api-keys),
which was the only time you could view the client secret.
If you did not save the client secret, you must generate a new client secret.

For example, run:

```sh
echo -n {clientId}:{clientSecret} | base64
```

Make a `POST` request to the
[cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token](generate-oauth2-token-atlas) endpoint to generate an access
token for your service account.

Replace `{base64Auth}` in the following example with the output
from the preceding step, then run:

**Input:**

```sh
curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'
```

**Output:**

```sh
{"access_token":"{accessToken}","expires_in":3600,"token_type":"Bearer"}%
```

> **Important:** The access token is valid for 1 hour (3600 seconds). You can't refresh an access token.
> When this access token expires, repeat this step to generate a new one.

Replace `{accessToken}` in the following example with the output from the
preceding step.

The following sample `GET` request
[returns all projects](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/listgroups) in your organization:

```sh
curl --request GET \
     --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups \
     --header 'Authorization: Bearer {accessToken}' \
     --header 'Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-02-01+json' \
     --header 'Content-Type: application/json'
```

The following sample `POST` request takes a request body and
[creates a project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/creategroup) named
`MyProject` in your organization:

```sh
curl --header 'Authorization: Bearer {accessToken}' \
      --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
      --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-02-01+json" \
      --include \
      --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups" \
      --data '
         {
            "name": "MyProject",
            "orgId": "5a0a1e7e0f2912c554080adc"
         }'
```

To make an API (Application Programming Interface) request using a service account, use the service account to generate
an access token, then use the access token in your request:

Locate the client secret beginning with `mdb_sa_sk_` that you saved
immediately after [when created](see-org-api-keys),
which was the only time you could view the client secret.
If you did not save the client secret, you must generate a new client secret.

For example, run:

```sh
echo -n {clientId}:{clientSecret} | base64
```

Make a `POST` request to the
[cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token](generate-oauth2-token-atlas) endpoint to generate an access
token for your service account.

Replace `{base64Auth}` in the following example with the output
from the preceding step, then run:

**Input:**

```sh
curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'
```

**Output:**

```sh
{"access_token":"{accessToken}","expires_in":3600,"token_type":"Bearer"}%
```

> **Important:** The access token is valid for 1 hour (3600 seconds). You can't refresh an access token.
> When this access token expires, repeat this step to generate a new one.

Replace `{accessToken}` in the following example with the output from the
preceding step.

The following sample `GET` request
[returns all projects](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/listgroups) in your organization:

```sh
curl --request GET \
     --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups \
     --header 'Authorization: Bearer {accessToken}' \
     --header 'Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-02-01+json' \
     --header 'Content-Type: application/json'
```

The following sample `POST` request takes a request body and
[creates a project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/creategroup) named
`MyProject` in your organization:

```sh
curl --header 'Authorization: Bearer {accessToken}' \
      --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
      --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2023-02-01+json" \
      --include \
      --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups" \
      --data '
         {
            "name": "MyProject",
            "orgId": "5a0a1e7e0f2912c554080adc"
         }'
```

Your request should resemble the following examples, where
`{PUBLIC-KEY}` is your API (Application Programming Interface) public key and `{PRIVATE-KEY}`
is the corresponding private key. To explore the available endpoints
through the Atlas Administration API, you can use the
[Postman workspace for MongoDB](https://www.postman.com/mongodb-devrel/workspace/mongodb-atlas-administration-apis/overview).

The following sample `GET` request
[returns all projects](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/listgroups) in your organization:

```sh
curl --user "{PUBLIC-KEY}:{PRIVATE-KEY}" --digest \
      --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
      --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-08-05+json" \
      --include \
      --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups"
```

The following sample `POST` request takes a request body and
[creates a project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/creategroup) named
`MyProject` in your organization:

```sh
curl --user "{PUBLIC-KEY}:{PRIVATE-KEY}" --digest \
      --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
      --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-08-05+json" \
      --include \
      --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups" \
      --data '
         {
            "name": "MyProject",
            "orgId": "5a0a1e7e0f2912c554080adc"
         }'
```

Your request should resemble the following examples, where
`{PUBLIC-KEY}` is your API (Application Programming Interface) public key and `{PRIVATE-KEY}`
is the corresponding private key. To explore the available endpoints
through the Atlas Administration API, you can use the
[Postman workspace for MongoDB](https://www.postman.com/mongodb-devrel/workspace/mongodb-atlas-administration-apis/overview).

The following sample `GET` request
[returns all projects](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/listgroups) in your organization:

```sh
curl --user "{PUBLIC-KEY}:{PRIVATE-KEY}" --digest \
      --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
      --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-08-05+json" \
      --include \
      --request GET "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups"
```

The following sample `POST` request takes a request body and
[creates a project](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/creategroup) named
`MyProject` in your organization:

```sh
curl --user "{PUBLIC-KEY}:{PRIVATE-KEY}" --digest \
      --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
      --header "Accept: application/vnd.atlas.2024-08-05+json" \
      --include \
      --request POST "https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/atlas/v2/groups" \
      --data '
         {
            "name": "MyProject",
            "orgId": "5a0a1e7e0f2912c554080adc"
         }'
```

> **See Also:** 
>
> - [Atlas Administration API Specification](https://mongodb.com/docs/atlas/reference/api-resources-spec)
>
> - [Atlas Administration API Error Codes](atlas-api-error-codes)

Alternatively, you can use any tool that supports [OpenAPI v3
specification](https://spec.openapis.org/oas/v3.0.1) to generate code
samples or mock servers. For example, you can import
the [Atlas Admin API Specification](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec)
into [Postman](https://www.postman.com/) to generate `curl`
commands.

> **Warning:** Using an HTTP URL with Postman will return a 301 status code as expected. However, in this scenario, Postman may automatically retry the request with HTTPS, but simultaneously strip the header and body from the retry request. This will return a 401 status code instead of 301, making it difficult to determine why the request failed.

To use Postman to generate `curl` commands:

codeBlocks.content:
curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'

curl --request POST \
   --url https://cloud.mongodb.com/api/oauth/token \
   --header 'accept: application/json' \
   --header 'cache-control: no-cache' \
   --header 'authorization: Basic {base64Auth}' \
   --header 'content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
   --data 'grant_type=client_credentials'

sections.heading: Step 1: In the MongoDB  Administration API , click  in the navigation bar.
Step 2: Right-click the OpenAPI specification format you want to download and copy the link.
Step 3: Open Postman and click , then .
sections.body: The **Import** window displays.

sections.heading: Step 4: In the  window, click  and paste the download link that you copied in step 2.
Step 5: Click , then .
Step 6: For the request for which you want to generate a  command, click  on the right and select  from the  dropdown.
Next Steps
sections.body:
To learn more about the Atlas Administration API, see
[Atlas Administration API Reference](atlas-admin-api-ref).

To manage programmatic access to the Atlas Administration API, see any of
the following procedures:

- [Manage Programmatic Access to an Organization](atlas-admin-api-access-org).

- [Manage Programmatic Access to Multiple Organizations](prog-access-mult-orgs).

- [Manage Programmatic Access to a Project](atlas-admin-api-access-project).

- [Rotate Service Account Secrets](rotate-service-account-secrets).
